blocks.html: Introduction A brief history of the Thai script The Thai writing system is believed to have been developed from Ancient Khmer script by the decree of King Ramkhamhaeng of Sukhothai in the year 1283. Prior to the creation of the Thai script, the Khmer script was used exclusively to transcribe Sanskrit and Pali from “Tripitaka” (the Buddhist equivalent of the Bible), but proved to be inadequate when used as a transcription of spoken Thai—it lacked the means to record tones (Khmer is not a tonal language) and lacked many Thai vowels. Thus the Thai script was invented to fulfil those needs. The modern Thai writing system, according to the Thai traditional grammar book “Lak Phasa Thai” (Phraya Uppakit Sinlapasan, 1968), consists of 44 consonant characters (2 of which are obsolete), 21 vowel symbols, which make up 32 vowel combinations (although it is doubtful that 4 of them can be considered “vowels”), 4 tone marks, and a number of punctuation marks. Why so many characters? The fact that there are 44 consonants doesn’t mean that there are 44 unique sounds, as there are only 20 consonant sounds in Thai (and a “zero” consonant which is linguistically described as a “glottal stop”). The reason many consonants share the same sound is that since the creation of the Thai writing system Thai has borrowed a vast number of words from Pali and Sanskrit, and at that time people made a huge effort to preserve the spelling of these “holy languages” although such a distinction between consonant sounds did not exist in Thai. As a result, many of the consonants share the same sound. It also explains why there are lots of consonants but only 8 possible final consonant sounds (e.g. [d], [t], [s], [j] and [ch] all sound like a very quiet [-t] at the end of syllables). How Thai script works A typical Thai syllable is composed of 2 to 4 constituents: initial consonant(s) and a vowel. Some also contain a final consonant and/or a tone mark: In Thai script, vowels aren’t always located after initial consonants. They may occur in front of, after, above, underneath, or enclosing the initial consonant(s) of the syllable. In each of the examples below, “ก” is a consonant and the remaining symbols are vowels. Note how each vowel’s position differs: One point worth mentioning: while front vowels and some enclosing vowels are placed in front of the initial consonants, it doesn’t mean they’re pronounced before the initial consonants. Don’t think of Thai writing in the linear fashion, think of Thai vowels as clip-on accessories. Vowels can tell you how to read The position of each vowel is fixed; some vowels are always placed in front, some vowels above, and so on. You can’t just put them wherever you want. On the other hand, since Thai is written without spaces between words (and that poses a small challenge because you need to find the word boundaries yourself), these vowel positions can give you clues about how to separate each word and syllable from one another e.g. if the vowel always occurs at the beginning of the syllable, it means that when you see that vowel, it marks the beginning of a new syllable, etc. Consonant acrophonics “Acrophony” is the naming of letters used in a writing system. Think of the Greek letters: “α” alpha, “β” beta, “γ” gamma, “δ” delta, where each letter is pronounced in ancient Greek as [a], [b], [g] and [d] respectively, while “alpha”, “beta”, “gamma” and “delta” are their names. This practice also exists in Thai. Each consonant is associated with a Thai word that contains that particular consonant. For instance, the name of the character “ก” is ก.ไก่ /gor gài/. ก. /gor/ represents the pronunciation of that consonant ([g] + inherent [-or] vowel) while ไก่ /gài/ demonstrates a word that contains the “ก” consonant, i.e. “chicken”. All (or at least most) literate native speakers of Thai know and can recite all of these consonant names. Knowing these is useful for when you ask someone to spell words for you, but you’re not sure which consonant character to use (e.g. “Do you mean [t] for ท.ทหาร /tor táhăan/ “soldier” or ธ.ธง /tor tong/ “flag”??). But since this book aims to teach you how to READ Thai in 10 days, trying to memorise all these names will only slow your progress down. However, if you’re eager to learn them anyway, you can read more about the consonant names in the Appendices at the end of this book. Sounds very complicated! Is it worth it? ABSOLUTELY YES. Learning a new script seems like a daunting task, but the benefits that come with it are immense. The fact that the Thai writing system is completely different from the Roman script-based writing system may deter beginners from learning to read Thai script, and just settle for phonetics. However, although the RTGS (Royal Thai General System of Transcription) is de facto the official system for transliterating Thai to the Roman alphabet (which you see on road signs, at train stations, etc.), this system has many flaws: it doesn’t show tones, it doesn’t differentiate between short and long vowels, it doesn’t even differentiate between several sound units (e.g. “ch” for both ช and จ, “o” for โ-, โ-ะ, -อ, and เ-าะ vowels. That’s 4 different vowels for one symbol!). So what are the perks of knowing Thai script other than showing it off as a party trick? 1. There’s no better system There is no standardised, universally understood Romanisation for Thai (even an official standardised Romanisation as we discussed earlier). There have been attempts by Thai language schools to create a perfect phonetic system for learners, but none have been successful so far. Besides, despite all your efforts to master any of these transliteration systems, it won’t be understood by most native speakers of Thai! Thais have the tendency to transliterate Thai into the Roman alphabet any way they want (it’s what they call “karaoke language”). For example, the word สวัสดี ‘hello’ can be written “sawaddee”, “sawutdii”, “sawasdee”, etc. depending on personal preference. Only Thai script is prevalent and consistent in Thailand. 2. It improves your pronunciation From reason #1, you can see how difficult it would be to improve your Thai pronunciation when you don’t even know how to pronounce it correctly! All transliteration systems are, at best, just rough guidelines. After relying on transliteration for too long you’ll inadvertently associate “j” with the J sound which doesn’t exist in Thai. When you see “a” you’ll be tempted to pronounce it like in the word “can” and not “ah” like it’s supposed to be pronounced. Once these associations are strong it’s very hard to change the way you speak, and you might end up stuck with a foreign accent forever. Thai script will remind you to pronounce words the way Thai people understand, and not with a “farang lisp”. 3. You’ll be very independent Don’t take English for granted. It won’t get you far in Thailand. In fact, it might not get you anywhere at all in some parts! Once you’re out of your comfort zone of Bangkok, pretty much everything is in Thai: restaurant menus, road signs, short instructions on how to use things like washing machines, etc. You might be fine if you’ve got a Thai person to help you, but do you want to be babied like that forever? Imagine how happy you’d feel if you could just go about your business by yourself. Independence tastes wonderful, I assure you! 4. It boosts your learning curve The biggest fear of all language learners is a plateau; you’re not improving any further. Once past the basics, you’re pretty much on your own in building vocabulary. If you don’t have constant stimuli encouraging you to learn more, you’ll hit that plateau soon enough. If you can read Thai, you’ll pick up words from things you encounter in your daily life: signs, billboards, books, magazines, and other texts. You can just learn on your own and your lexical bank will accumulate slowly but surely with each passing day with no great effort. Who can be your best teacher, if not yourself? Disclaimers regarding this book There are a few things I want us to agree upon before we begin: 1. Have a realistic expectation “Read Thai in 10 Days” is definitely feasible. After you finish this book you’ll be equipped with sufficient reading skills to understand different Thai texts around you, given that you study for 10 days without fail and master each chapter before you move on. But just like any other skill, expertise comes with time and practice. It’s only natural that you will make lots of mistakes in the beginning, so be patient and keep a positive attitude. Most importantly, never stop practising. Always reinforce your skills by trying to read all kinds of materials available to you. I’ll help you break the first barrier, but after that you need to be your own hero! 2. Ditch the pronunciation guide FAST I’ve included transliteration throughout the book which you can quickly refer to whenever doubts arise. However, you have to stand on your own two feet and abandon these phonetics as soon as possible. Hiding behind the comfort of a pronunciation guide will get your reading skill nowhere and eventually damage your pronunciation. Rather, you should try to consult the audio recordings for precise pronunciation. If you wish to use the audio recordings, you can download them from this url: www.ReadThaiLanguage.com/getaudio You will need to submit a form and soon you’ll receive a link to the files. Don’t worry, this process won’t take long! 3. You are not learning to read the way Thais do You don’t speak Thai as your first language. The Thais do. Thus your mindset regarding the language is a world away from theirs. Learning to read Thai the Thai way, while it may build a stronger foundation, is a much longer process (well, we spend six years in primary school!) and only works best when your Thai is already up to par. In this book, I propose a shortcut so you can readily go out in the world and start reading things, even if you only know a little Thai. Native speakers of Thai may be surprised by the approach of this book, e.g. “Why don’t you learn the names of the consonants??”, “What kind of tone rules are those??”, “เ-ย [-eui] is not a real vowel!”, etc. but don’t be alarmed that the way you will be learning is not the way Thais have. Whichever road you take, as long as you get to the destination, all is well. Over the next few pages, I’ll walk you through the pronunciation guide, but if you want to use the audio recordings as your only guide (available at www.ReadThaiLanguage.com/getaudio), feel free to skim through it, or even skip it. As you wish. How to use this book There are 10 chapters from “DAY 1” to “LAST DAY”. I advise you to study only one chapter per day, at this rate you should finish the whole book within 2 weeks. Continuity is crucial in learning. Leave too big a gap and you’ll forget everything. Also, please do not cram more than one chapter into a day either. If you put too much pressure on your brain, it will push back and refuse to learn anything new! For most of the exercises, the pronunciation answer key is provided in a link next to the track number for the exercise in the form of the phonetic system used in this book. However, if you want a more accurate way to check your pronunciation, use the audio recordings instead. (and I’d actually prefer that you do that!) At the end of each chapter make sure you understand all the checkpoints in the “Today’s Recap” section. If you don’t understand any of the points, go back and review them again. It’s very important to consolidate what you know, so don’t forget to review! How much time should you spend each day depends on how much you understand the lesson. So if you find any lesson particularly difficult and you’re losing the energy to carry on, don’t let it make you give up. Take a day off and come back again the next day. Remember, this is not a competition. You’re doing this for your own benefit, so always be kind to yourself.
